title: Management Quiz
description: Patient Rights, Ethics, Care Coordination, Legal Regulations, Leadership

questions.text: A nurse is creating a care plan for a patient and prioritizes addressing the patient's acute pain over their need for nutritional education. This prioritization is primarily based on which framework?
options: The facility's clinical pathway for the diagnosis.
questions.explanation: Consider a well-known psychological theory that ranks human needs in order of importance.

questions.text: A patient with decision-making capacity informs the nurse that they wish to refuse a recommended surgery. This decision is protected under which ethical principle?
questions.explanation: This ethical principle emphasizes a person's right to self-governance and making their own choices.

questions.text: During a handoff report, a nurse states, 'The patient's blood pressure has been trending downward, and I am concerned about potential hypovolemia.' Which part of the SBAR technique does this statement represent?
questions.explanation: This part of the communication tool involves the caregiver's analysis of the situation.

questions.text: A hospital must decide which of two equally needy patients receives a single available organ for transplant. This scenario most directly involves the ethical principle of:
questions.explanation: Consider the ethical principle concerned with fairness and allocation of resources.

questions.text: A nurse manager who makes decisions independently and strictly enforces rules, leaving team members with little input, is demonstrating which leadership style?
questions.explanation: This leadership style is often compared to that of a military commander who expects orders to be followed.

questions.text: Which of the following is considered one of the 'Five Rights of Delegation' in nursing?
questions.explanation: This principle of delegation focuses on ensuring the individual receiving the task is competent and qualified.

questions.text: A patient recovering from a stroke no longer requires hospitalization but is not yet able to perform daily activities independently at home. Which level of care is most appropriate for this patient?
questions.explanation: This level of care serves as a bridge between the intensive environment of a hospital and returning home.

questions.text: What is the primary function of the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)?
options: To set minimum safety standards in the workplace.
To regulate the standards and quality of care in nursing homes.
To protect the privacy of an individual's healthcare information.
To ensure emergency treatment is provided regardless of ability to pay.
questions.explanation: This federal law is primarily associated with patient confidentiality.

questions.text: A grateful patient's family offers a nurse a valuable piece of jewelry. According to professional boundaries, what is the most appropriate action?
options: Politely decline, explaining that the care provided is part of the professional role.
Suggest they make a donation to the hospital foundation instead.
Accept the gift on behalf of the entire unit to be shared later.
Accept the gift to avoid offending the family.
questions.explanation: Consider the need to maintain a professional relationship and avoid any appearance of obligation.

questions.text: The care delivery model where an interdisciplinary team, including nurses, therapists, and social workers, collaboratively provides care to a group of patients is known as:
questions.explanation: This model emphasizes teamwork and coordination across different healthcare professions for a group of patients.

questions.text: Which federal law requires hospitals to provide medical screening and stabilization to any patient presenting to an emergency department, regardless of their ability to pay?
options: EMTALA (Emergency Medical Treatment and Active Labor Act)
questions.explanation: This law is often referred to as the anti-dumping law for emergency departments.

questions.text: In the context of research, what is the purpose of a control group?
options: To conduct a survey of the literature before the study begins.
To receive the treatment or intervention being evaluated.
To serve as a baseline for comparison by not receiving the treatment.
To ensure the sample size is large enough for statistical significance.
questions.explanation: This group provides a point of reference to measure the effect of the intervention.

questions.text: A nurse who administers a fatal dose of medication due to a calculation error has committed which type of tort?
questions.explanation: This legal term applies when harm is caused by a failure to act with reasonable care, not by a deliberate act to cause harm.

questions.text: A 70-year-old individual needs coverage for prescription drugs. Which part of Medicare should they enroll in?
questions.explanation: Think about the letter of the Medicare program specifically designed for pharmaceuticals.

questions.text: A hospital uses a system to classify patients based on their diagnosis for the purpose of determining how much the hospital should be reimbursed by Medicare. This system is known as:
questions.explanation: This billing system groups patients who share similar diagnoses and treatments.

questions.text: The process of collecting patient data through observation, interviews, and physical examination occurs during which step of the nursing process?
questions.explanation: This is the first and foundational step in the systematic approach to patient care.

questions.text: A nurse is working with a patient and their family to incorporate their cultural and spiritual beliefs into the plan of care. This action primarily addresses which factor affecting care planning?
questions.explanation: This aspect of care planning recognizes that a patient's background and belief systems significantly influence their health experiences.

questions.text: A nurse calls a physician to report a change in a patient's condition. The physician gives a new order over the phone. What is a critical step the nurse must take to ensure patient safety?
options: Tell the physician to enter the order in the computer immediately.
Implement the order immediately to ensure timely care.
Ask a colleague to listen to the order simultaneously.
Write down the order, then read it back to the physician for confirmation.
questions.explanation: This safety procedure is designed to close the communication loop and prevent errors from misinterpretation.

questions.text: What is the main goal of conflict resolution in a healthcare team setting?
options: To temporarily suppress the disagreement to maintain a quiet work environment.
To ensure the leader's initial opinion is adopted by the team.
To identify which team member was at fault.
To produce a positive outcome by opening dialogue and seeking a solution.
questions.explanation: The objective is not to win an argument, but to improve teamwork and care delivery.

questions.text: A case manager is coordinating a patient's discharge. This role primarily involves which activity?
options: Diagnosing the patient's primary medical condition.
Performing direct, hands-on nursing tasks like wound care.
Coordinating services and resources to fulfill the patient's healthcare needs.
Prescribing medications needed for the patient after discharge.
questions.explanation: This professional acts as a central point of contact to ensure a smooth transition of care.

questions.text: The use of telehealth has expanded to provide care to patients remotely. Which of the following is a primary benefit of telehealth?
options: It guarantees complete privacy with no risk of data breaches.
It lowers the overall cost of healthcare and increases patient access.
It allows for hands-on physical assessments and procedures.
It is most effective for managing acute, life-threatening emergencies.
questions.explanation: Consider the advantages of providing healthcare services without the patient needing to travel.

questions.text: Which type of research study is considered the 'gold standard' for testing the efficacy of a new treatment due to its design that minimizes bias?
questions.explanation: This study design involves randomly assigning participants to different groups to compare outcomes.

questions.text: A nurse is reviewing a patient's medical record. Which document would provide a chronological account of the patient's care, condition, and response to treatment?
questions.explanation: These entries are made regularly by doctors, nurses, and therapists to document the patient's journey.

questions.text: In evidence-based practice, what is the highest level of evidence?
options: Evidence supported by suggestive clinical studies with a theoretical basis.
Evidence from descriptive or comparative studies.
Evidence from well-designed experimental studies like RCTs.
questions.explanation: This level of evidence comes from the most rigorous type of scientific study designed to eliminate bias.

questions.text: A nurse notices that several patients have developed pressure ulcers after being placed on a new type of mattress. The first step the nurse should take as part of the quality improvement process is to:
options: Implement a new wound care protocol immediately.
Collect data on all patients with pressure ulcers in the hospital.
Identify the situation as a potential problem that could benefit from change.
questions.explanation: Think about the very first action one must take before attempting to solve any problem.

title: Safety & Infection Control
description: Patient Safety, Disaster Management, Hazardous Materials, Infection Control

questions.text: A nurse is caring for a client who requires contact precautions. Which of the following actions should the nurse take?
options: Wear a mask when entering the room.
Wear a gown and gloves when entering the room.
Keep the door to the client's room closed at all times.
Ensure the client wears a mask when family visits.
questions.explanation: Contact precautions require gown and gloves to prevent transmission via direct contact with the patient or contaminated surfaces.

questions.text: Which isolation precaution requires a negative pressure room?
questions.explanation: Airborne precautions require a negative pressure room to prevent airborne pathogens (TB, measles, varicella) from escaping.

questions.text: A client is prescribed a restraint. How often should the nurse release the restraint for assessment?
questions.explanation: Restraints must be released at least every 2 hours to assess circulation, skin integrity, and provide range of motion.

questions.text: Which of the following is the most effective method for preventing the spread of infection?
options: Wearing sterile gloves for all procedures
questions.explanation: Hand hygiene is the single most effective method to prevent pathogen transmission in healthcare settings.

questions.text: What is the correct order for doffing (removing) PPE?
questions.explanation: Remove most contaminated items first: Gloves, then Goggles/Face Shield, then Gown, and finally Mask (least contaminated).

questions.text: R.A.C.E. fire response protocol stands for:
questions.explanation: R.A.C.E.: Rescue clients in immediate danger, Alarm (activate fire alarm), Contain the fire (close doors), Extinguish if safe.

questions.text: During a disaster, a client arrives with a green tag. What does this indicate?
options: Immediate life-threatening injuries requiring urgent care
Delayed treatment - stable injuries that can wait
questions.explanation: Green tag = Minor injuries, walking wounded. These patients can wait and are treated last in disaster triage.

questions.text: A client with tuberculosis requires which type of mask?
options: No mask needed if room door is closed
questions.explanation: TB requires airborne precautions with an N95 respirator (or PAPR) to filter airborne particles smaller than 5 microns.

questions.text: Which client requires droplet precautions?
questions.explanation: Influenza requires droplet precautions (surgical mask, within 3 feet). TB and chickenpox need airborne; C. diff needs contact precautions.

questions.text: A nurse discovers a mercury spill from a broken thermometer. What is the priority action?
options: Evacuate the area and restrict access
Wipe up mercury with paper towels
Pour water over the spill to dilute it
questions.explanation: Mercury is hazardous. Priority is to evacuate, restrict access, and call the hazardous materials team. Never vacuum mercury.

questions.text: Which of The Joint Commission's National Patient Safety Goals addresses patient identification?
options: Use at least two patient identifiers before administering medications
questions.explanation: Using two patient identifiers (name and DOB, not room number) before procedures/medications is a core safety goal.

questions.text: A client is at high risk for falls. Which intervention is most appropriate?
options: Place the bed in the lowest position
Keep the side rails fully raised at all times
questions.explanation: Bed in lowest position reduces fall injury risk. Restraints increase fall risk; full side rails are considered restraints.

questions.text: During disaster triage, which color tag indicates the highest priority for treatment?
questions.explanation: Red tag = Immediate, life-threatening injuries requiring urgent intervention (e.g., airway obstruction, severe bleeding).

questions.text: A nurse is caring for a client with C. difficile. Which precaution is essential?
options: Use alcohol-based hand sanitizer after contact
Wash hands with soap and water
Place client in negative pressure room
questions.explanation: C. diff spores are not killed by alcohol. Soap and water hand washing is required, along with contact precautions.

questions.text: P.A.S.S. fire extinguisher technique stands for:
questions.explanation: P.A.S.S.: Pull the pin, Aim at base of fire, Squeeze the handle, Sweep side to side.

questions.text: Which client requires airborne precautions?
questions.explanation: Measles requires airborne precautions (N95, negative pressure room). MRSA needs contact; pertussis needs droplet precautions.

questions.text: A nurse witnesses a chemical spill in the hallway. What is the first action?
options: Alert others and evacuate the area
questions.explanation: Safety first: Alert others, evacuate the area, and activate the hazardous materials response team.

questions.text: Standard precautions should be used:
options: Only for clients with known infections
For all clients at all times
questions.explanation: Standard precautions are used for ALL clients, ALL the time, regardless of diagnosis, to prevent transmission of pathogens.

questions.text: A client is receiving chemotherapy. How should the nurse handle the client's linens?
options: Double-bag in specially marked hazardous waste bags
Wash separately in hot water only
questions.explanation: Linens contaminated with chemotherapy require double-bagging in specially marked hazardous waste bags per facility protocol.

questions.text: Which factor increases a client's risk for falls?
questions.explanation: Diuretics increase fall risk due to frequent urination (rushing to bathroom), orthostatic hypotension, and electrolyte imbalances.

questions.text: During a mass casualty event, which client should be triaged as yellow (delayed)?
options: Open fracture of the femur, stable vital signs
questions.explanation: Yellow = Delayed. Serious injuries but stable vital signs; can wait for treatment (e.g., fractures without hemorrhage).

questions.text: A nurse is preparing to administer a medication and notices the client's ID band is missing. What should the nurse do?
options: Ask the client to state their name and proceed
Check the room number and proceed
Obtain a new ID band before administering medication
Ask the roommate to verify the client's identity
questions.explanation: Two identifiers are required. A new ID band must be obtained before medication administration to ensure correct patient identification.

questions.text: Which PPE is required for standard precautions when there is risk of blood splash?
options: Gloves, gown, mask, and eye protection
questions.explanation: Risk of splash to face/eyes requires full PPE: gloves, gown, mask, and eye protection (goggles or face shield).

questions.text: A client with varicella (chickenpox) requires which precautions?
questions.explanation: Varicella requires both airborne (N95, negative pressure) and contact precautions due to airborne transmission and skin lesions.

questions.text: When should a nurse perform hand hygiene? Select the BEST answer.
options: Only before and after direct patient contact
Before and after glove use, and between patients
Only when hands are visibly soiled
At the beginning and end of each shift
questions.explanation: Hand hygiene is required: before/after patient contact, before/after glove use, after touching contaminated surfaces, between patients.

title: Health Promotion & Maintenance Part 1
description: Pediatrics, Growth & Development, Immunizations, Newborn Care

questions.text: At what age should an infant double their birth weight?
questions.explanation: Infants typically double their birth weight by 6 months and triple it by 1 year.

questions.text: According to Erikson's theory, what is the primary developmental task for infants (0-1 year)?
questions.explanation: Erikson's first stage (0-1 year) is Trust vs. Mistrust, where infants learn to trust caregivers to meet their needs.

questions.text: A newborn has an APGAR score of 8 at 1 minute. What does this indicate?
options: Good condition with minimal intervention needed
questions.explanation: APGAR 7-10 = Good condition. Score of 8 is normal; minor interventions like stimulation or oxygen may be needed.

questions.text: At what age should a child receive the MMR (measles, mumps, rubella) vaccine?
questions.explanation: MMR is given at 12-15 months (first dose) and 4-6 years (second dose). It's a live vaccine, not given before 12 months.

questions.text: Which reflex should be present in a healthy newborn?
questions.explanation: All are normal newborn reflexes. Babinski (toes fan), Moro (startle), and gag reflex should all be present.

questions.text: According to Piaget, a 2-year-old child is in which cognitive stage?
questions.explanation: Preoperational stage (2-7 years) is characterized by symbolic thinking, egocentrism, and magical thinking.

questions.text: What is the most important injury prevention measure for infants?
options: Never leave infant unattended on elevated surfaces
Use a car seat facing forward
Allow infant to sleep on stomach
questions.explanation: Falls are a major injury risk. Never leave infants unattended on changing tables, beds, or couches.

questions.text: At what age can a child typically ride a tricycle?
questions.explanation: By age 3, most children have the gross motor skills and coordination to ride a tricycle.

questions.text: Which vaccine is given at birth?
questions.explanation: Hepatitis B vaccine is given at birth (first dose), then at 1-2 months and 6-18 months.

questions.text: A 4-month-old infant should be able to:
questions.explanation: By 4 months, infants can typically roll from back to front. Sitting unsupported occurs around 6 months.

questions.text: What is the recommended sleep position for infants to prevent SIDS?
questions.explanation: 'Back to sleep' reduces SIDS risk. Infants should sleep on their backs on a firm surface without loose bedding.

questions.text: According to Erikson, what is the developmental task for toddlers (1-3 years)?
questions.explanation: Toddlers (1-3 years) develop autonomy through independence and self-control. Encouraging choices supports this stage.

questions.text: At what age should solid foods be introduced to an infant?
questions.explanation: Solid foods are introduced around 4-6 months when the infant shows readiness (sits with support, shows interest in food).

questions.text: Which finding in a newborn requires immediate intervention?
questions.explanation: Central cyanosis indicates inadequate oxygenation and requires immediate intervention. Acrocyanosis is normal in newborns.

questions.text: The DTaP vaccine protects against which diseases?
questions.explanation: DTaP = Diphtheria, Tetanus, and acellular Pertussis (whooping cough). Given at 2, 4, 6, 15-18 months, and 4-6 years.

questions.text: A 6-month-old infant should be able to:
questions.explanation: By 6 months, infants can typically sit without support. Walking occurs around 12 months; first words around 12 months.

questions.text: What is the appropriate car seat position for an infant under 2 years?
options: No car seat needed if held by adult
questions.explanation: Infants and toddlers under 2 years should be rear-facing in the back seat for optimal safety.

questions.text: Which developmental milestone is expected at 12 months?
questions.explanation: By 12 months, children typically say 2-3 words. Running occurs around 18-24 months; drawing circles around 3 years.

questions.text: What is the normal respiratory rate for a newborn?
questions.explanation: Normal newborn respiratory rate is 30-60 breaths/min. Rates above 60 may indicate respiratory distress.

questions.text: According to Freud's theory, a 2-year-old is in which psychosexual stage?
questions.explanation: Anal stage (1-3 years) focuses on toilet training and control. Oral stage is 0-1 year; phallic is 3-6 years.

questions.text: Which food should NOT be given to infants under 1 year?
questions.explanation: Honey can contain botulism spores and should not be given to infants under 1 year due to immature immune systems.

questions.text: What is the expected head circumference growth in the first year of life?
questions.explanation: Head circumference increases approximately 12 cm (5 inches) in the first year, then slows significantly.

questions.text: At what age should a child be able to speak in 2-3 word sentences?
questions.explanation: By 24 months (2 years), children typically use 2-3 word sentences. Vocabulary is around 50-300 words.

questions.text: Which vaccine is a live attenuated vaccine and should NOT be given to immunocompromised children?
questions.explanation: MMR is a live vaccine and contraindicated in immunocompromised children. DTaP, Hep B, and Hib are inactivated.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of well-child visits?
options: Health promotion, disease prevention, and developmental screening
questions.explanation: Well-child visits focus on health promotion, disease prevention, growth monitoring, developmental screening, and immunizations.

questions.text: A 3-year-old child should be able to:
questions.explanation: By age 3, children can copy a circle. Tying shoes occurs around 5-6 years; writing name around 4-5 years.

questions.text: What is the most common cause of death in children ages 1-4 years?
questions.explanation: Unintentional injuries (drowning, motor vehicle accidents, falls, poisoning) are the leading cause of death in this age group.

questions.text: Which reflex disappears by 4 months of age?
questions.explanation: Rooting reflex (turns head toward touch on cheek) disappears around 4 months. Gag, blink, and cough are protective reflexes that persist.

questions.text: According to Erikson, what is the developmental task for preschoolers (3-6 years)?
questions.explanation: Preschoolers (3-6 years) develop initiative through play, exploration, and asking questions. Encourage creativity and curiosity.

questions.text: What is the recommended method for taking a temperature in a newborn?
questions.explanation: Rectal temperature is most accurate for newborns and infants under 3 months. Axillary is acceptable for screening.

title: Psychosocial Integrity
description: Therapeutic Communication, Abuse Recognition, Grief, Mental Health

questions.text: Which therapeutic communication technique is best for a grieving client?
questions.explanation: Silence and active listening allow the client to express feelings without judgment or pressure, facilitating the grief process.

questions.text: According to Kübler-Ross, what is the first stage of grief?
questions.explanation: The five stages of grief are: Denial, Anger, Bargaining, Depression, and Acceptance (DABDA).

questions.text: A nurse suspects child abuse. What is the priority action?
options: Report to appropriate authorities per facility protocol
Document suspicions in the chart only
questions.explanation: Nurses are mandated reporters. Suspected abuse must be reported immediately to child protective services or appropriate authorities.

questions.text: Which statement is an example of therapeutic communication?
options: Tell me more about how you're feeling.
questions.explanation: Open-ended questions like 'Tell me more...' encourage the client to express feelings and thoughts. 'Why' questions can be defensive.

questions.text: A client says 'I'm going to kill myself.' What is the nurse's priority response?
options: Ask if the client has a specific plan
Tell the client not to talk that way
Reassure the client things will get better
questions.explanation: Assess suicide risk by asking about plan, means, and intent. Never leave a suicidal client alone; stay and call for help.

questions.text: Which finding is most indicative of elder abuse?
options: Multiple bruises in various stages of healing
questions.explanation: Bruises in various stages of healing suggest repeated trauma and are a red flag for physical abuse.

questions.text: A client in the anger stage of grief yells at the nurse. What is the best response?
options: Tell the client to calm down
Remain calm and allow the client to express feelings
questions.explanation: Anger is a normal grief stage. Remain calm, set boundaries if needed, and allow safe expression of feelings.

questions.text: Which communication technique should be AVOIDED?
questions.explanation: False reassurance (e.g., 'Everything will be fine') dismisses client concerns and blocks therapeutic communication.

questions.text: A client with depression says 'I'm worthless.' What is the therapeutic response?
options: Why do you feel that way?
You sound like you're feeling pretty low about yourself.
questions.explanation: Reflection acknowledges feelings without judgment. Avoid 'why' questions and false reassurance.

questions.text: Which behavior suggests a child is being sexually abused?
questions.explanation: Age-inappropriate sexual knowledge or behavior is a strong indicator of sexual abuse and requires immediate reporting.

questions.text: A client in the bargaining stage of grief might say:
options: This can't be happening to me.
If I pray more, maybe I'll get better.
I just feel so sad all the time.
questions.explanation: Bargaining involves attempting to negotiate (often with a higher power) to change the outcome.

questions.text: Which is a priority assessment for a client with suicidal ideation?
questions.explanation: Assessing access to lethal means (guns, pills, etc.) is critical for safety. Remove access to means if possible.

questions.text: A nurse observes a parent yelling and threatening a child. What should the nurse do?
options: Ignore it as a private family matter
Intervene calmly and assess the situation
questions.explanation: Intervene to ensure child safety, assess the situation, and report per protocol. Emotional abuse is reportable.

questions.text: Which statement demonstrates empathy?
options: I know exactly how you feel.
This must be very difficult for you.
questions.explanation: Empathy acknowledges the client's feelings without claiming to know exactly how they feel or minimizing their experience.

questions.text: A client with anxiety is hyperventilating. What is the priority intervention?
options: Have client breathe into a paper bag
questions.explanation: Coach slow, deep breathing to reduce hyperventilation. Paper bags are no longer recommended due to hypoxia risk.

questions.text: Which is a sign of domestic violence?
options: Partner answers all questions for the client
questions.explanation: Controlling behavior (partner answering for client, not allowing privacy) is a red flag for domestic violence.

questions.text: In the acceptance stage of grief, a client might say:
options: I can't believe this is real.
Why is this happening to me?
I'm ready to face what's ahead.
If only I had done things differently.
questions.explanation: Acceptance involves coming to terms with reality and finding peace with the situation.

questions.text: A client with schizophrenia says 'The TV is sending me messages.' What is the therapeutic response?
options: I don't hear any messages, but I understand this is real to you.
questions.explanation: Acknowledge the client's reality without reinforcing the delusion. Don't argue or agree with delusions.

questions.text: Which is a risk factor for suicide?
questions.explanation: Previous suicide attempt is the strongest predictor of future attempts. Other risks: depression, substance abuse, isolation, access to means.

questions.text: A nurse suspects a colleague is impaired by substances. What should the nurse do?
options: Report to the nurse manager per facility policy
questions.explanation: Patient safety is priority. Report suspected impairment to the nurse manager or appropriate authority per facility protocol.

questions.text: Which communication technique involves restating the client's message?
questions.explanation: Paraphrasing restates the client's message in the nurse's own words to ensure understanding.

questions.text: A client with PTSD is experiencing a flashback. What is the priority intervention?
options: Orient the client to the present
questions.explanation: During flashbacks, orient the client to the present using grounding techniques (e.g., 'You are safe. You are in the hospital.').

questions.text: Which is a sign of neglect in an elderly client?
questions.explanation: Poor hygiene, malnutrition, untreated medical conditions, and unsafe living conditions indicate neglect.

questions.text: A client says 'I feel like I'm going crazy.' What is the best response?
options: Tell me what you mean by that.
questions.explanation: Clarification helps understand the client's meaning. Avoid false reassurance or dismissing feelings.

questions.text: Which is an appropriate intervention for a client with depression?
options: Allow the client to sleep all day
Tell the client to cheer up
questions.explanation: Encourage activity and socialization. Depression worsens with isolation and inactivity.

questions.text: A client with mania is pacing and talking rapidly. What is the priority intervention?
questions.explanation: Reduce stimulation to decrease agitation. Use short, simple statements. Ensure safety.

questions.text: Which behavior suggests a therapeutic nurse-client relationship?
options: Visiting the client at home after discharge
questions.explanation: Professional boundaries protect both nurse and client. Avoid dual relationships and maintain therapeutic focus.

questions.text: A client with anorexia nervosa is exercising excessively. What should the nurse do?
options: Allow exercise as a coping mechanism
Set limits on exercise per treatment plan
Encourage more exercise to build appetite
questions.explanation: Excessive exercise is a compensatory behavior in eating disorders. Set limits and monitor per treatment plan.

questions.text: Which is a therapeutic response to a client who is crying?
options: Don't cry, things will get better.
questions.explanation: Therapeutic presence (sitting quietly, offering tissues) shows support without interrupting the emotional release.

questions.text: A client with alcohol withdrawal is experiencing hallucinations. What is the priority?
options: Ensure safety and administer prescribed medications
questions.explanation: Alcohol withdrawal can cause delirium tremens with hallucinations. Ensure safety, administer benzodiazepines as ordered, monitor vital signs.

questions.text: Which statement indicates a client is in the depression stage of grief?
options: I can't believe this is happening.
I just want to be left alone. Nothing matters anymore.
If I change my lifestyle, maybe I'll recover.
questions.explanation: Depression stage involves sadness, withdrawal, and feelings of hopelessness about the loss.

questions.text: A client with OCD is washing hands repeatedly. What is the appropriate intervention?
options: Allow the behavior initially, then gradually set limits
questions.explanation: Don't abruptly stop compulsions (increases anxiety). Gradually set limits while teaching alternative coping strategies.

questions.text: Which is a priority when caring for a client in restraints?
options: Leave restraints on continuously for 8 hours
Document only at the end of shift
questions.explanation: Monitor circulation, skin integrity, and vital signs every 15-30 minutes. Release restraints every 2 hours for ROM and toileting.

questions.text: A client says 'I hear voices telling me to hurt myself.' What is the priority action?
options: Ask what the voices are saying
Ensure immediate safety and stay with the client
Tell the client the voices aren't real
questions.explanation: Command hallucinations to harm self or others require immediate safety measures. Stay with client, remove dangerous objects, notify provider.

questions.text: Which intervention promotes a therapeutic environment for mental health clients?
options: Allowing clients to do whatever they want
questions.explanation: Structure, consistency, and clear expectations provide safety and reduce anxiety in therapeutic environments.

title: Basic Care and Comfort
description: Wound Care, Pressure Injuries, Mobility, Elimination, Comfort

questions.text: To prevent pressure ulcers, the nurse should turn the client every:
questions.explanation: Turning every 2 hours is the standard of care to relieve pressure and preserve skin integrity.

questions.text: A pressure injury with full-thickness skin loss and visible subcutaneous fat is classified as:
questions.explanation: Stage 3 involves full-thickness skin loss with visible fat. Muscle, tendon, and bone are NOT visible (that's Stage 4).

questions.text: Which nutrient is most important for wound healing?
questions.explanation: Protein is essential for tissue repair and wound healing. Vitamin C and zinc also support healing.

questions.text: A client has a Braden Scale score of 12. What does this indicate?
questions.explanation: Braden Scale: ≤12 = High risk, 13-14 = Moderate risk, 15-18 = Low risk, 19-23 = No risk. Lower scores = higher risk.

questions.text: When assisting a client to ambulate, where should the nurse stand?
questions.explanation: Stand on the weaker side to provide support and prevent falls. Use a gait belt for safety.

questions.text: A Stage 2 pressure injury is characterized by:
options: Partial-thickness skin loss with exposed dermis
Full-thickness skin loss with visible fat
Full-thickness tissue loss with exposed bone
questions.explanation: Stage 2 = Partial-thickness loss with exposed dermis, may appear as blister or shallow ulcer. Dermis is pink/red.

questions.text: Which intervention promotes bowel elimination in a bedridden client?
questions.explanation: Increase fiber, fluids, and activity (as tolerated) to promote bowel elimination. Avoid routine laxative use.

questions.text: What is the priority when caring for a client with a urinary catheter?
options: Empty the drainage bag when full
Keep the drainage bag below bladder level
Disconnect and reconnect tubing as needed
questions.explanation: Keep drainage bag below bladder level to prevent backflow and infection. Never disconnect tubing; maintain closed system.

questions.text: A client reports pain of 8/10. What is the priority intervention?
questions.explanation: Severe pain (8/10) requires immediate intervention. Administer medication, then reassess effectiveness in 30-60 minutes.

questions.text: Which finding indicates a Stage 1 pressure injury?
questions.explanation: Stage 1 = Intact skin with non-blanchable redness (doesn't turn white when pressed). Skin may be warm and firm.

questions.text: When transferring a client from bed to wheelchair, what should the nurse do first?
options: Raise the bed to highest position
questions.explanation: Lock wheelchair brakes first for safety. Then position wheelchair at 45° angle to bed on client's stronger side.

questions.text: A wound with red, moist tissue and granulation is in which phase of healing?
questions.explanation: Proliferative phase shows granulation tissue (red, moist, bumpy). This is healthy healing tissue.

questions.text: Which assistive device provides the most support for ambulation?
questions.explanation: Walker provides maximum stability and support. Cane provides least support; crutches require upper body strength.

questions.text: What is the correct technique for measuring a wound?
options: Measure length x width x depth
questions.explanation: Measure length (head to toe), width (side to side), and depth (deepest point) in centimeters. Document consistently.

questions.text: A client with constipation should avoid which food?
questions.explanation: Cheese and other dairy products can worsen constipation. Encourage high-fiber foods, fluids, and activity.

questions.text: Which intervention reduces the risk of catheter-associated UTI?
questions.explanation: Perineal care with soap and water reduces infection risk. Maintain closed system; don't irrigate routinely or disconnect tubing.

questions.text: A client uses a cane. Which instruction is correct?
options: Move cane and stronger leg together
questions.explanation: Hold cane on stronger side. Move cane and weaker leg together, then stronger leg. Cane should be 6 inches to side.

questions.text: What is the purpose of a pressure-relieving mattress?
options: Distribute pressure evenly to prevent skin breakdown
questions.explanation: Pressure-relieving devices (foam, air, gel mattresses) distribute pressure to prevent pressure injuries in high-risk clients.

questions.text: Which complementary therapy is appropriate for pain management?
questions.explanation: Complementary therapies (guided imagery, music, massage, heat/cold) can enhance pain relief when used with medications.

questions.text: A client has a Stage 4 pressure injury. What is visible in the wound?
questions.explanation: Stage 4 = Full-thickness tissue loss with exposed muscle, tendon, or bone. High risk for osteomyelitis.

questions.text: When should postmortem care be performed?
questions.explanation: Perform postmortem care promptly after death is pronounced. Allow family time with body first if desired, respecting cultural practices.

questions.text: A client is on bedrest. Which intervention prevents complications?
options: Keep client flat at all times
questions.explanation: ROM exercises prevent contractures and maintain joint mobility. Also turn q2h, encourage deep breathing, and prevent DVT.

questions.text: Which wound requires a sterile dressing change?
questions.explanation: Surgical incisions require sterile technique. Chronic wounds (pressure injuries) use clean technique after initial healing.

questions.text: A client reports bladder fullness but cannot void. What is the priority?
questions.explanation: Assess for urinary retention by palpating bladder. May need catheterization if unable to void and bladder is distended.

questions.text: What is the correct position for a client receiving an enema?
questions.explanation: Left lateral (Sims') position allows enema solution to flow along natural curve of sigmoid colon and rectum.

title: Pharmacological Therapies Part 1
description: Medication Administration, CV, Respiratory, Endocrine, Neuro Drugs

questions.text: Nitroglycerin is given for chest pain. Which side effect is most common?
questions.explanation: Headache is a common side effect due to vasodilation. Nitroglycerin causes hypotension and tachycardia, not hypertension or bradycardia.

questions.text: A client is taking warfarin (Coumadin). Which lab value should the nurse monitor?
questions.explanation: PT/INR monitors warfarin effectiveness. Therapeutic INR is typically 2-3. aPTT monitors heparin.

questions.text: Which medication requires checking apical pulse before administration?
questions.explanation: Check apical pulse before digoxin. Hold if pulse <60 bpm (adult) due to risk of bradycardia and heart block.

questions.text: A client taking furosemide (Lasix) should be monitored for:
questions.explanation: Furosemide is a loop diuretic that causes potassium loss. Monitor for hypokalemia; encourage potassium-rich foods or supplements.

questions.text: Which drug suffix indicates a beta blocker?
questions.explanation: -lol = beta blockers (metoprolol, atenolol). -pril = ACE inhibitors, -statin = cholesterol drugs, -dipine = calcium channel blockers.

questions.text: A client on lisinopril (ACE inhibitor) develops a dry cough. What should the nurse do?
questions.explanation: Dry cough is a common side effect of ACE inhibitors. Notify provider; client may need to switch to an ARB (angiotensin receptor blocker).

questions.text: What are the 'Five Rights' of medication administration?
options: Right patient, drug, dose, route, time
Right patient, drug, doctor, route, time
Right patient, drug, dose, room, time
Right patient, drug, dose, route, documentation
questions.explanation: Five Rights: Right patient, drug, dose, route, and time. Some add: right documentation, right reason, right to refuse.

questions.text: A client is prescribed albuterol inhaler. What is the primary action?
questions.explanation: Albuterol is a beta-2 agonist bronchodilator for quick relief of bronchospasm. Corticosteroids reduce inflammation.

questions.text: Which insulin has the fastest onset?
questions.explanation: Rapid-acting insulins (lispro, aspart) have onset in 10-15 minutes. Give immediately before meals. Regular onset is 30-60 min.

questions.text: A client taking levothyroxine (Synthroid) for hypothyroidism should take it:
options: On an empty stomach in the morning
questions.explanation: Take levothyroxine on empty stomach 30-60 minutes before breakfast for best absorption. Avoid taking with calcium or iron.

questions.text: Which is a sign of digoxin toxicity?
questions.explanation: Digoxin toxicity signs: visual changes (halos), nausea, bradycardia, arrhythmias. Therapeutic level: 0.5-2 ng/mL.

questions.text: A client is prescribed metoprolol (beta blocker). Which condition is a contraindication?
questions.explanation: Beta blockers can cause bronchoconstriction and are contraindicated in asthma/COPD. Also avoid in heart block and bradycardia.

questions.text: Which medication should be given with food to reduce GI upset?
questions.explanation: NSAIDs like ibuprofen should be taken with food to reduce GI irritation and ulcer risk.

questions.text: A client using an inhaled corticosteroid should:
options: Use it for acute asthma attacks
questions.explanation: Rinse mouth after inhaled corticosteroids to prevent oral thrush. Use bronchodilator first, then corticosteroid. Not for acute attacks.

questions.text: What is the antidote for heparin overdose?
questions.explanation: Protamine sulfate reverses heparin. Vitamin K reverses warfarin. Naloxone reverses opioids. Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines.

questions.text: A client is prescribed NPH insulin. When is the peak action?
questions.explanation: NPH (intermediate-acting) peaks at 4-12 hours. Monitor for hypoglycemia during peak. Glargine/detemir have no peak.

questions.text: Which lab value indicates therapeutic effect of warfarin?
questions.explanation: Therapeutic INR for warfarin is 2-3 (may be 2.5-3.5 for mechanical heart valves). INR 0.8-1.2 is normal/subtherapeutic.

questions.text: A client taking amlodipine (calcium channel blocker) should avoid:
questions.explanation: Grapefruit juice increases calcium channel blocker levels, causing excessive hypotension. Avoid grapefruit with many medications.

questions.text: Which route has the fastest absorption?
questions.explanation: IV has immediate onset (fastest). Then: IM, SubQ, oral (slowest). Sublingual and inhalation are also rapid.

questions.text: A client is prescribed prednisone (corticosteroid). Which side effect should the nurse monitor?
questions.explanation: Corticosteroids cause hyperglycemia, hypertension, weight gain, immunosuppression, and osteoporosis with long-term use.

questions.text: Which medication requires a peak and trough level?
questions.explanation: Aminoglycosides (gentamicin, tobramycin) require peak/trough monitoring due to narrow therapeutic window and nephrotoxicity/ototoxicity risk.

questions.text: A client is prescribed metformin for diabetes. What is a serious side effect?
questions.explanation: Lactic acidosis is a rare but serious side effect of metformin. Hold before contrast dye procedures. Metformin alone doesn't cause hypoglycemia.

questions.text: Which drug class ends in '-pril'?
questions.explanation: -pril = ACE inhibitors (lisinopril, enalapril). Monitor for dry cough, hyperkalemia, and angioedema.

questions.text: A client is prescribed spironolactone (Aldactone). The nurse should monitor for:
questions.explanation: Spironolactone is a potassium-sparing diuretic. Monitor for hyperkalemia. Avoid potassium supplements and salt substitutes.

questions.text: What is the antidote for warfarin overdose?
questions.explanation: Vitamin K (phytonadione) reverses warfarin. IV for severe bleeding, PO for minor. Protamine reverses heparin.

questions.text: A client is prescribed phenytoin (Dilantin) for seizures. Which side effect is common?
questions.explanation: Phenytoin causes gingival hyperplasia (gum overgrowth). Encourage good oral hygiene. Also causes hirsutism and ataxia.

questions.text: Which insulin is long-acting with no peak?
questions.explanation: Glargine and detemir are long-acting (24 hours) with no peak. Provide basal coverage. Cannot be mixed with other insulins.

questions.text: A client taking hydrochlorothiazide (HCTZ) should increase intake of:
questions.explanation: HCTZ is a thiazide diuretic causing potassium loss. Encourage potassium-rich foods (bananas, oranges, potatoes).

questions.text: Which medication should NOT be crushed?
questions.explanation: Never crush enteric-coated or sustained-release medications. Crushing destroys the coating and alters absorption.

questions.text: A client is prescribed nitroglycerin sublingual. How should it be taken?
questions.explanation: Sublingual nitroglycerin dissolves under tongue for rapid absorption. Can repeat every 5 minutes x3. Call 911 if pain persists.

questions.text: Which adverse effect is associated with ACE inhibitors?
questions.explanation: ACE inhibitors can cause hyperkalemia by reducing aldosterone. Also cause dry cough and angioedema (rare but serious).

questions.text: A client is prescribed albuterol and fluticasone inhalers. Which should be used first?
questions.explanation: Use bronchodilator first to open airways, then corticosteroid for better penetration. Wait 1-2 minutes between inhalers.

questions.text: Which medication requires monitoring of liver function tests?
questions.explanation: Acetaminophen is hepatotoxic in high doses or chronic use. Monitor LFTs. Maximum dose: 4g/day (3g/day for elderly or liver disease).

questions.text: A client is prescribed enoxaparin (Lovenox). What is the route of administration?
questions.explanation: Enoxaparin (low molecular weight heparin) is given subcutaneously. Do not aspirate or massage injection site. Rotate sites.

questions.text: Which statement indicates a client understands insulin teaching?
options: I'll store insulin in the freezer.
I'll reuse needles to save money.
I'll skip doses if I'm not eating.
questions.explanation: Rotate injection sites to prevent lipodystrophy. Don't freeze insulin. Use new needles. Don't skip long-acting insulin even if not eating.

questions.text: A client taking atorvastatin (statin) reports muscle pain. What should the nurse do?
questions.explanation: Muscle pain may indicate rhabdomyolysis (rare but serious). Notify provider. Monitor CK levels. May need to discontinue statin.

questions.text: Which medication should be taken 30 minutes before breakfast?
questions.explanation: Alendronate (bisphosphonate) taken 30-60 min before breakfast with full glass of water. Remain upright 30 min to prevent esophageal irritation.

questions.text: A client is prescribed morphine. Which vital sign is priority to monitor?
questions.explanation: Opioids cause respiratory depression. Monitor respiratory rate; hold if <12/min. Have naloxone (Narcan) available for overdose.

questions.text: Which drug class ends in '-dipine'?
questions.explanation: -dipine = calcium channel blockers (amlodipine, nifedipine). Cause vasodilation, reduce BP. Monitor for peripheral edema, headache.

questions.text: A client is prescribed regular insulin and NPH insulin. Which should be drawn up first?
questions.explanation: Draw up clear (regular) before cloudy (NPH). Mnemonic: 'Clear before cloudy' or 'RN' (Regular before NPH).

title: Pharmacological Therapies Part 2
description: Pain Management, GI, Psychiatric, Geriatric Pharmacology

questions.text: When administering lithium, the nurse monitors for toxicity. Early signs include:
questions.explanation: Early lithium toxicity: fine tremors, nausea, polyuria. Advanced toxicity: coarse tremors, vomiting, confusion, seizures. Therapeutic level: 0.6-1.2 mEq/L.

questions.text: What is the antidote for opioid overdose?
questions.explanation: Naloxone reverses opioid effects including respiratory depression. May need repeated doses. Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines.

questions.text: A client taking an SSRI (selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor) should be monitored for:
questions.explanation: Serotonin syndrome symptoms: agitation, confusion, tremor, hyperthermia, muscle rigidity. Can occur with SSRI overdose or drug interactions.

questions.text: Which pain scale is appropriate for a 4-year-old child?
questions.explanation: FACES scale (Wong-Baker) is appropriate for children 3+ years. Shows faces from happy to crying. Numeric scales work for ages 8+.

questions.text: A client is prescribed morphine PCA (patient-controlled analgesia). What is the priority assessment?
questions.explanation: Monitor respiratory rate closely with opioids. Hold if <12/min. Also assess sedation level, pain relief, and for side effects.

questions.text: Which medication is used to treat extrapyramidal symptoms (EPS) from antipsychotics?
questions.explanation: Benztropine (anticholinergic) treats EPS (dystonia, akathisia, pseudoparkinsonism) caused by typical antipsychotics.

questions.text: A client taking omeprazole (proton pump inhibitor) for GERD should take it:
questions.explanation: PPIs work best when taken 30 minutes before first meal of the day on empty stomach for maximum acid suppression.

questions.text: Which is a common side effect of opioid analgesics?
questions.explanation: Opioids commonly cause constipation. Prevent with stool softeners, fluids, fiber. Also cause nausea, sedation, respiratory depression.

questions.text: A client is prescribed lorazepam (Ativan) for anxiety. Which statement indicates understanding?
options: I can drink alcohol while taking this.
I should avoid driving until I know how this affects me.
I can stop taking this abruptly.
questions.explanation: Benzodiazepines cause sedation. Avoid alcohol and driving. Don't stop abruptly (risk of seizures). Can be habit-forming.

questions.text: Which antiemetic is contraindicated in Parkinson's disease?
questions.explanation: Metoclopramide blocks dopamine and can worsen Parkinson's symptoms. Ondansetron (5-HT3 antagonist) is safe alternative.

questions.text: What is the maximum daily dose of acetaminophen for adults?
questions.explanation: Maximum acetaminophen: 4g/day for healthy adults, 3g/day for elderly or liver disease. Exceeding causes hepatotoxicity.

questions.text: A client taking haloperidol (typical antipsychotic) develops muscle rigidity, fever, and altered mental status. This indicates:
questions.explanation: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) is life-threatening: muscle rigidity, hyperthermia, altered consciousness, autonomic instability. Stop drug immediately.

questions.text: Which laxative works by drawing water into the intestine?
questions.explanation: Osmotic laxatives (PEG, lactulose, magnesium citrate) draw water into bowel. Gentler than stimulant laxatives.

questions.text: A geriatric client is at higher risk for medication toxicity due to:
questions.explanation: Elderly have decreased kidney/liver function, increased body fat, decreased lean mass. Start low, go slow with dosing.

questions.text: Which SSRI has the longest half-life?
questions.explanation: Fluoxetine has longest half-life (4-6 days). Can discontinue without tapering. Other SSRIs require gradual taper to avoid withdrawal.

questions.text: A client is prescribed fentanyl patch for chronic pain. When does it reach peak effect?
questions.explanation: Fentanyl patch takes 12-24 hours to reach peak. Change every 72 hours. Dispose properly (fold sticky sides together).

questions.text: Which medication requires monitoring for tardive dyskinesia?
questions.explanation: Tardive dyskinesia (involuntary movements) occurs with long-term antipsychotic use, especially typical antipsychotics. May be irreversible.

questions.text: A client taking an MAOI (monoamine oxidase inhibitor) should avoid:
questions.explanation: Avoid tyramine-rich foods with MAOIs (aged cheese, wine, cured meats) - can cause hypertensive crisis. Rarely used due to dietary restrictions.

questions.text: Which pain medication is contraindicated in clients with peptic ulcer disease?
questions.explanation: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) increase ulcer risk and GI bleeding. Acetaminophen is safer for clients with ulcers.

questions.text: A client is prescribed ondansetron (Zofran) for chemotherapy-induced nausea. When should it be given?
questions.explanation: Give antiemetics 30 minutes before chemotherapy for prevention. More effective than treating nausea after it occurs.

questions.text: Which statement about tramadol is correct?
questions.explanation: Tramadol is a weak opioid that lowers seizure threshold. Avoid in seizure disorders. Has lower respiratory depression risk than morphine.

questions.text: A client taking clozapine (atypical antipsychotic) requires monitoring of:
questions.explanation: Clozapine requires WBC monitoring (agranulocytosis risk), blood glucose (diabetes risk), and LFTs. Most effective but highest risk antipsychotic.

questions.text: Which medication is used for opioid-induced constipation?
questions.explanation: Stool softeners (docusate) and stimulant laxatives prevent/treat opioid-induced constipation. Start prophylactically with opioid therapy.

questions.text: A client is prescribed gabapentin for neuropathic pain. Which statement is correct?
options: It is an anticonvulsant used for nerve pain
questions.explanation: Gabapentin is an anticonvulsant effective for neuropathic pain (diabetic neuropathy, postherpetic neuralgia). Taper gradually to discontinue.

questions.text: Which antacid should be avoided in clients with kidney disease?
questions.explanation: Magnesium-containing antacids can cause hypermagnesemia in kidney disease. Aluminum antacids can cause constipation.

questions.text: A client taking duloxetine (Cymbalta) for depression and pain should be monitored for:
questions.explanation: SNRIs (duloxetine, venlafaxine) can increase blood pressure. Monitor BP regularly. Also effective for neuropathic pain and fibromyalgia.

questions.text: Which medication is first-line for acute migraine?
questions.explanation: Triptans (sumatriptan) are first-line for acute migraine. Contraindicated in coronary artery disease. NSAIDs are alternative.

questions.text: A geriatric client should avoid which medication class due to fall risk?
questions.explanation: Benzodiazepines increase fall risk in elderly due to sedation and impaired coordination. Use cautiously or avoid. Consider alternatives.

questions.text: Which is a sign of anticholinergic toxicity?
options: Dry mouth, blurred vision, urinary retention
questions.explanation: Anticholinergic toxicity: 'Dry as a bone, blind as a bat, red as a beet, hot as a hare, mad as a hatter.' Causes dry mouth, blurred vision, urinary retention, hyperthermia, confusion.

questions.text: A client is prescribed bisacodyl (Dulcolax). What is the mechanism of action?
questions.explanation: Bisacodyl is a stimulant laxative that increases intestinal motility. Works in 6-12 hours orally, 15-60 min rectally. Don't use long-term.

questions.text: Which pain assessment tool is appropriate for a nonverbal client?
questions.explanation: FLACC (Face, Legs, Activity, Cry, Consolability) assesses pain in nonverbal clients. Also use behavioral indicators: grimacing, guarding, restlessness.

questions.text: A client taking risperidone (atypical antipsychotic) should be monitored for:
questions.explanation: Atypical antipsychotics cause weight gain, hyperglycemia, dyslipidemia (metabolic syndrome). Monitor weight, glucose, lipids regularly.

questions.text: Which medication is used to reverse benzodiazepine overdose?
questions.explanation: Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines. Use cautiously - can precipitate seizures in chronic users. Naloxone reverses opioids.

questions.text: A client is prescribed sucralfate (Carafate) for peptic ulcer. When should it be taken?
questions.explanation: Sucralfate forms protective barrier over ulcer. Take 1 hour before meals and at bedtime on empty stomach. Don't take with antacids.

questions.text: Which is a Beers Criteria medication to avoid in elderly?
questions.explanation: Beers Criteria lists potentially inappropriate medications for elderly. Diphenhydramine (anticholinergic) causes confusion, falls, urinary retention. Avoid in elderly.

title: Reduction of Risk & Cardiovascular
description: Lab Values, Diagnostics, CV Monitoring, MI, CHF, Stroke

questions.text: What is the normal range for serum potassium?
questions.explanation: Normal potassium: 3.5-5.0 mEq/L. <3.5 = hypokalemia, >5.0 = hyperkalemia. Critical values require immediate intervention.

questions.text: A client with chest pain has elevated troponin levels. This indicates:
questions.explanation: Elevated troponin (cardiac enzyme) indicates myocardial damage/MI. Troponin is more specific than CK-MB for cardiac injury.

questions.text: What is the normal range for hemoglobin in adult males?
questions.explanation: Normal Hgb: Males 14-18 g/dL, Females 12-16 g/dL. Low Hgb indicates anemia; high Hgb may indicate polycythemia or dehydration.

questions.text: A client is experiencing an acute MI. What is the priority intervention?
questions.explanation: MONA protocol for MI: Morphine (pain), Oxygen (if SpO2 <90%), Nitroglycerin (chest pain), Aspirin (antiplatelet). Also obtain 12-lead ECG immediately.

questions.text: What is the therapeutic INR range for a client on warfarin?
questions.explanation: Therapeutic INR: 2-3 for most indications (may be 2.5-3.5 for mechanical valves). <2 = subtherapeutic, >4 = bleeding risk.

questions.text: A client with heart failure has crackles in lungs and peripheral edema. What medication is priority?
questions.explanation: Diuretics (furosemide) reduce fluid overload in CHF. Also use ACE inhibitors, beta blockers, and monitor daily weights.

questions.text: What is the normal range for serum sodium?
questions.explanation: Normal sodium: 135-145 mEq/L. <135 = hyponatremia, >145 = hypernatremia. Affects neurological and cardiac function.

questions.text: A client post-cardiac catheterization should be monitored for:
questions.explanation: Post-cath: Monitor insertion site for bleeding/hematoma, check distal pulses, keep leg straight 4-6 hours, watch for contrast reaction and kidney function.

questions.text: What is the normal range for platelet count?
questions.explanation: Normal platelets: 150,000-400,000/mm³. <50,000 = bleeding risk, <20,000 = spontaneous bleeding risk. Implement bleeding precautions.

questions.text: A client with hypertension should limit sodium intake to:
questions.explanation: Recommended sodium: <2,300 mg/day for general population, <1,500 mg/day for hypertension. DASH diet emphasizes fruits, vegetables, low-fat dairy.

questions.text: What is a normal fasting blood glucose level?
questions.explanation: Normal fasting glucose: 70-100 mg/dL. 100-125 = prediabetes, ≥126 = diabetes. Random glucose ≥200 with symptoms also indicates diabetes.

questions.text: A client is having a stroke. What assessment tool should the nurse use?
questions.explanation: FAST: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call 911. Quick stroke screening tool. Also use NIH Stroke Scale for detailed assessment.

questions.text: What is the normal range for serum creatinine?
questions.explanation: Normal creatinine: 0.6-1.2 mg/dL. Elevated creatinine indicates kidney dysfunction. Also monitor BUN (normal 8-20 mg/dL).

questions.text: A client with atrial fibrillation is at risk for:
questions.explanation: A-fib causes blood pooling in atria, forming clots that can embolize to brain (stroke). Anticoagulation (warfarin, DOACs) prevents stroke.

questions.text: What is the normal range for WBC count?
questions.explanation: Normal WBC: 4,500-11,000/mm³. <4,500 = leukopenia (infection risk), >11,000 = leukocytosis (infection, inflammation, leukemia).

questions.text: A client post-MI should avoid which activity?
questions.explanation: Avoid Valsalva maneuver (bearing down, straining) - increases cardiac workload. Prevent constipation with stool softeners. Gradual activity increase.

questions.text: What is the normal range for serum calcium?
questions.explanation: Normal calcium: 8.5-10.5 mg/dL. Hypocalcemia causes tetany, seizures. Hypercalcemia causes kidney stones, confusion, cardiac arrhythmias.

questions.text: A client with left-sided heart failure will have:
questions.explanation: Left-sided HF: Pulmonary congestion (crackles, dyspnea, orthopnea). Right-sided HF: Peripheral edema, JVD, hepatomegaly, ascites.

questions.text: What is a critical low hemoglobin value requiring immediate intervention?
questions.explanation: Hgb <7 g/dL is critical - may need transfusion. Symptoms: fatigue, pallor, tachycardia, dyspnea. Notify provider immediately.

questions.text: A client is prescribed nitroglycerin for angina. What should the nurse teach?
options: Take up to 3 doses, 5 minutes apart
questions.explanation: Sublingual NTG: Take 1 dose, repeat q5min x3 if needed. Call 911 if pain persists after 3 doses. Store in dark bottle, causes headache (normal).

questions.text: What is the normal range for serum magnesium?
questions.explanation: Normal magnesium: 1.5-2.5 mEq/L. Hypomagnesemia causes tremors, arrhythmias. Hypermagnesemia causes decreased reflexes, respiratory depression.

questions.text: A client with peripheral arterial disease should:
questions.explanation: PAD: Keep legs dependent (down) to improve arterial flow. Avoid crossing legs, tight clothing. Elevate legs for venous disease, not arterial.

questions.text: What is the normal range for HbA1c?
questions.explanation: Normal HbA1c: <5.7%. 5.7-6.4% = prediabetes, ≥6.5% = diabetes. HbA1c reflects average glucose over 2-3 months.

questions.text: A client is receiving thrombolytic therapy for stroke. What is the priority assessment?
questions.explanation: Monitor BP closely with thrombolytics - keep systolic <180 mmHg to prevent hemorrhagic stroke. Also monitor for bleeding (major complication).

questions.text: What is the normal range for BUN (blood urea nitrogen)?
questions.explanation: Normal BUN: 8-20 mg/dL. Elevated BUN indicates kidney dysfunction or dehydration. BUN:Creatinine ratio helps differentiate causes.

questions.text: A client with deep vein thrombosis should:
options: Keep leg elevated and immobile initially
questions.explanation: DVT: Elevate leg, bed rest initially, anticoagulation. Never massage (can dislodge clot causing PE). Gradual ambulation after anticoagulation started.

questions.text: What is a normal PT (prothrombin time)?
questions.explanation: Normal PT: 11-13 seconds. PT/INR monitors warfarin. aPTT (25-35 sec) monitors heparin. Don't confuse the two.

questions.text: A client with heart failure should be taught to:
questions.explanation: Daily weights detect fluid retention (report gain of 2-3 lbs in 1 day or 5 lbs in 1 week). Restrict sodium and fluids as ordered.

questions.text: What is the normal range for serum albumin?
questions.explanation: Normal albumin: 3.5-5.0 g/dL. Low albumin indicates malnutrition, liver disease, or kidney disease. Affects wound healing and fluid balance.

questions.text: A client post-pacemaker insertion should avoid:
questions.explanation: Avoid MRI (can damage pacemaker). Modern pacemakers are safe with microwaves, cell phones. Avoid raising arm above shoulder on pacemaker side for 4-6 weeks.

questions.text: What is the normal range for aPTT (activated partial thromboplastin time)?
questions.explanation: Normal aPTT: 25-35 seconds. Therapeutic on heparin: 60-80 seconds (1.5-2x normal). aPTT monitors heparin, not warfarin.

questions.text: A client with angina should be taught to:
options: Stop activity and rest if pain occurs
questions.explanation: Angina: Stop activity, rest, take nitroglycerin. If pain persists after 3 NTG doses (15 min), call 911 - may be MI.

questions.text: What is the normal range for total cholesterol?
questions.explanation: Desirable total cholesterol: <200 mg/dL. 200-239 = borderline high, ≥240 = high. Also monitor LDL (<100), HDL (>40 men, >50 women), triglycerides (<150).

questions.text: A client with hypertensive crisis (BP 220/120) should receive:
questions.explanation: Hypertensive crisis requires IV antihypertensive (nitroprusside, labetalol) for rapid BP reduction. Monitor for end-organ damage (stroke, MI, kidney failure).

questions.text: What is the normal range for serum glucose (random)?
questions.explanation: Normal random glucose: <140 mg/dL. ≥200 with symptoms indicates diabetes. Fasting glucose is more accurate for diagnosis.

questions.text: A client with venous insufficiency should:
questions.explanation: Venous insufficiency: Elevate legs above heart to improve venous return. Wear compression stockings. Opposite of arterial disease.

questions.text: What is a critical high potassium level?
questions.explanation: Critical hyperkalemia: >6.5 mEq/L. Causes cardiac arrhythmias, can be fatal. ECG changes: peaked T waves, widened QRS. Give calcium gluconate, insulin/glucose, kayexalate.

questions.text: A client is having a cardiac catheterization. What allergy is most important to assess?
questions.explanation: Assess shellfish/iodine allergy - contrast dye contains iodine. Also assess kidney function (contrast is nephrotoxic). Hydrate before and after procedure.

questions.text: What is the normal range for serum chloride?
questions.explanation: Normal chloride: 95-105 mEq/L. Usually follows sodium. Hypochloremia occurs with vomiting; hyperchloremia with dehydration.

questions.text: A client with cardiogenic shock will have:
questions.explanation: Cardiogenic shock: Decreased cardiac output, hypotension, pulmonary edema. Caused by MI, severe HF. Treat with inotropes, diuretics, possible mechanical support.

questions.text: What is the normal range for RBC count in adult males?
questions.explanation: Normal RBC: Males 4.5-5.5 million/mm³, Females 4.0-5.0 million/mm³. Low RBC indicates anemia; high RBC may indicate polycythemia.

questions.text: A client with stable angina experiences chest pain:
questions.explanation: Stable angina: Predictable chest pain with exertion, relieved by rest/NTG. Unstable angina: Pain at rest, increasing frequency/severity (pre-MI).

questions.text: What is the normal range for hematocrit in adult males?
questions.explanation: Normal Hct: Males 42-52%, Females 37-47%. Hct is % of RBCs in blood. Low Hct indicates anemia; high Hct indicates polycythemia or dehydration.

questions.text: A client post-CABG (coronary artery bypass graft) should avoid:
questions.explanation: Post-CABG: Avoid driving 4-6 weeks (sternum healing). Do cough/deep breathe, use incentive spirometer. Splint chest with pillow. Gradual activity increase.

questions.text: What is a critical low platelet count?
questions.explanation: Critical thrombocytopenia: <20,000/mm³ (spontaneous bleeding risk). <50,000 = bleeding precautions. Avoid IM injections, rectal temps, aspirin.

title: Respiratory, Neurological & Endocrine
description: COPD, Asthma, Stroke, Seizures, Diabetes, Thyroid, ABGs

questions.text: A client with COPD should be positioned:
questions.explanation: High Fowler's (sitting upright) maximizes lung expansion and eases breathing in COPD. Also use pursed-lip breathing.

questions.text: What oxygen level is appropriate for a client with COPD?
questions.explanation: COPD clients need low-flow oxygen (1-3 L/min) to maintain hypoxic drive. High oxygen can suppress respiratory drive.

questions.text: A client having a tonic-clonic seizure. What is the priority action?
options: Protect from injury and turn to side
questions.explanation: Protect from injury, turn to side (prevents aspiration), loosen clothing. Never restrain or put anything in mouth. Time the seizure.

questions.text: Which ABG indicates respiratory acidosis?
questions.explanation: Respiratory acidosis: pH <7.35, PaCO2 >45 (retaining CO2). Causes: COPD, hypoventilation, respiratory depression.

questions.text: A client with diabetes has blood glucose of 45 mg/dL. What should the nurse do?
questions.explanation: Hypoglycemia (<70 mg/dL): Give 15g simple carbs (juice, glucose tablets), recheck in 15 min. If unconscious, give glucagon or D50W IV.

questions.text: A client with asthma is wheezing. Which medication provides quickest relief?
questions.explanation: Albuterol (beta-2 agonist) provides quick relief of bronchospasm. Corticosteroids reduce inflammation but take hours/days to work.

questions.text: Which sign indicates increased intracranial pressure?
questions.explanation: Cushing's triad (late ICP sign): Widening pulse pressure, bradycardia, irregular respirations. Also: decreased LOC, headache, vomiting, dilated pupils.

questions.text: A client with hypothyroidism will have:
questions.explanation: Hypothyroidism: Weight gain, bradycardia, fatigue, cold intolerance, constipation, depression. Treat with levothyroxine.

questions.text: Which ABG indicates metabolic alkalosis?
questions.explanation: Metabolic alkalosis: pH >7.45, HCO3 >26. Causes: vomiting, NG suction, excess antacids, diuretics.

questions.text: A client with pneumonia should:
options: Cough and deep breathe every 2 hours
questions.explanation: Encourage coughing/deep breathing, incentive spirometer, fluids (thins secretions), ambulation. Monitor for respiratory distress.

questions.text: A client post-stroke has left-sided weakness. Where should the nurse stand during ambulation?
questions.explanation: Stand on the weaker side to provide support and prevent falls. Use gait belt for safety.

questions.text: Which finding indicates DKA (diabetic ketoacidosis)?
options: Blood glucose 600 mg/dL with fruity breath
questions.explanation: DKA: Hyperglycemia (>250), ketones, fruity breath, Kussmaul respirations, dehydration. Treat with IV insulin and fluids.

questions.text: A client with COPD is receiving oxygen. Which finding is concerning?
questions.explanation: Respiratory rate <12 indicates respiratory depression from too much oxygen (suppressed hypoxic drive). Reduce oxygen, stimulate breathing.

questions.text: Which medication prevents seizures?
questions.explanation: Anticonvulsants (phenytoin, valproic acid, carbamazepine) prevent seizures. Monitor drug levels, don't stop abruptly.

questions.text: A client with hyperthyroidism will have:
questions.explanation: Hyperthyroidism (Graves' disease): Weight loss, tachycardia, heat intolerance, exophthalmos, tremors, anxiety. Treat with antithyroid drugs or radioactive iodine.

questions.text: Which ABG indicates respiratory alkalosis?
questions.explanation: Respiratory alkalosis: pH >7.45, PaCO2 <35 (blowing off CO2). Causes: hyperventilation, anxiety, pain, fever.

questions.text: A client with asthma should avoid:
questions.explanation: Avoid asthma triggers: smoke, allergens, cold air, exercise (without pre-medication). Use peak flow meter to monitor.

questions.text: A client post-stroke has difficulty swallowing. What is the priority?
questions.explanation: Dysphagia increases aspiration risk. Keep NPO until swallow study. Elevate HOB 30-90°, thicken liquids if cleared.

questions.text: Which sign indicates hypoglycemia?
questions.explanation: Hypoglycemia: Shakiness, sweating, tachycardia, confusion, hunger. Treat immediately with 15g simple carbs. Hyperglycemia: polyuria, polydipsia, dry skin.

questions.text: Which ABG indicates metabolic acidosis?
questions.explanation: Metabolic acidosis: pH <7.35, HCO3 <22. Causes: DKA, renal failure, diarrhea, lactic acidosis.

questions.text: A client with COPD should use which breathing technique?
questions.explanation: Pursed-lip breathing: Inhale through nose, exhale slowly through pursed lips. Prevents airway collapse, improves gas exchange.

questions.text: A client with increased ICP should avoid:
questions.explanation: Avoid Valsalva, hip flexion, coughing (increases ICP). Elevate HOB 30°, keep head midline, prevent constipation, minimize stimulation.

questions.text: Which is a sign of thyroid storm?
questions.explanation: Thyroid storm (hyperthyroid crisis): Severe tachycardia, hyperthermia, agitation, delirium. Life-threatening. Give antithyroid drugs, beta blockers, cooling measures.

questions.text: A client with pneumonia has rusty-colored sputum. This indicates:
questions.explanation: Rusty/blood-tinged sputum suggests bacterial pneumonia (Streptococcus pneumoniae). Green sputum = Pseudomonas. Treat with antibiotics.

questions.text: A client post-seizure is confused and sleepy. This is called:
questions.explanation: Postictal state: Confusion, drowsiness, headache after seizure. Provide safety, reorient, allow rest. Document seizure characteristics.

questions.text: Which insulin should be given for DKA?
questions.explanation: DKA requires regular insulin IV drip (only insulin that can be given IV). Also give IV fluids, monitor potassium, treat underlying cause.

questions.text: A client with asthma has a peak flow reading in the red zone. This means:
questions.explanation: Peak flow zones: Green (80-100% = good), Yellow (50-80% = caution), Red (<50% = emergency). Red zone requires immediate treatment.

questions.text: Which position is best for a client with increased ICP?
questions.explanation: Elevate HOB 30° to promote venous drainage and reduce ICP. Keep head midline (avoid neck flexion/rotation).

questions.text: A client with diabetes is NPO for surgery. What should the nurse do with morning insulin?
options: Give half dose or per protocol
questions.explanation: NPO: Usually give half dose long-acting insulin or per facility protocol. Monitor glucose closely. Don't give rapid-acting insulin without food.

questions.text: Which finding indicates status epilepticus?
questions.explanation: Status epilepticus: Continuous seizure >5 min or repeated seizures without regaining consciousness. Medical emergency. Give IV benzodiazepines.

questions.text: A client with COPD should receive which vaccine?
questions.explanation: COPD clients need pneumococcal vaccine and annual flu vaccine to prevent respiratory infections.

questions.text: Which cranial nerve is assessed by having the client smile and frown?
questions.explanation: CN VII (Facial) controls facial expressions. Bell's palsy affects CN VII causing facial drooping. Stroke can also cause facial asymmetry.

questions.text: Which is a sign of hyperglycemia?
questions.explanation: Hyperglycemia: 3 P's (Polyuria, Polydipsia, Polyphagia), blurred vision, fatigue, dry skin. Chronic hyperglycemia causes complications.

questions.text: A client with pneumonia has decreased breath sounds in right lower lobe. This indicates:
questions.explanation: Decreased/absent breath sounds indicate consolidation, fluid, or collapsed lung. Pneumonia causes consolidation. Also assess for dullness to percussion.

questions.text: Which is the first step in ABG interpretation?
questions.explanation: ABG steps: 1) pH (acidosis <7.35 or alkalosis >7.45), 2) PaCO2 (respiratory), 3) HCO3 (metabolic), 4) Determine if compensated.

questions.text: A client with stroke has aphasia. This means:
questions.explanation: Aphasia: Language impairment. Expressive = can't speak, Receptive = can't understand, Global = both. Dysphagia = swallowing difficulty.

questions.text: Which medication treats hyperthyroidism?
questions.explanation: Antithyroid drugs (methimazole, PTU) treat hyperthyroidism. Also: radioactive iodine or thyroidectomy. Levothyroxine treats hypothyroidism.

questions.text: A client with asthma is using a metered-dose inhaler. What should the nurse teach?
options: Shake inhaler, exhale, inhale slowly while pressing canister
questions.explanation: MDI technique: Shake, exhale fully, press canister while inhaling slowly, hold breath 10 seconds. Use spacer for better delivery.

questions.text: Which is a late sign of increased ICP?
questions.explanation: Early ICP signs: Headache, nausea, restlessness, decreased LOC. Late signs: Cushing's triad (widening pulse pressure, bradycardia, irregular respirations), dilated pupils.

questions.text: A client with diabetes has HbA1c of 9%. This indicates:
questions.explanation: HbA1c goal: <7% for most diabetics. 9% indicates poor control over past 2-3 months. Adjust medications, reinforce diet/exercise.

title: Immunology, Hematology & GI
description: HIV/AIDS, Anemia, Transfusions, Ulcers, IBD, Liver Disease

questions.text: A client with iron-deficiency anemia should increase intake of:
options: Red meat and dark leafy greens
questions.explanation: Iron-rich foods: red meat, dark leafy greens, beans. Take with vitamin C (citrus) to enhance absorption. Avoid taking with calcium/dairy.

questions.text: A client is receiving a blood transfusion and develops fever, chills, and back pain. What should the nurse do first?
questions.explanation: Fever, chills, back pain indicate transfusion reaction. Stop transfusion immediately, keep IV open with NS, notify provider, monitor vitals, save blood bag.

questions.text: A client with peptic ulcer disease should avoid:
questions.explanation: Avoid NSAIDs (cause ulcers), alcohol, caffeine, spicy foods. Milk provides temporary relief but increases acid production later.

questions.text: Which lab value indicates pernicious anemia?
questions.explanation: Pernicious anemia: B12 deficiency due to lack of intrinsic factor. Causes neurological symptoms. Treat with B12 injections.

questions.text: A client with Crohn's disease will have:
questions.explanation: Crohn's: Skip lesions (patchy), anywhere in GI tract, fistulas/abscesses common. Ulcerative colitis: continuous inflammation, colon/rectum only.

questions.text: A client with HIV has a CD4 count of 180. This indicates:
